title: First & Last Occurrence
code: public static int[] SearchRange(int[] nums, int target)

frames.caption: Goal: bracket the run of 8s. Step 1 — LowerBound(8) finds the FIRST index i with nums[i] >= 8.
frames.note: LowerBound finds the FIRST index i with nums[i] >= target — the left edge of the duplicate run. Its half-open interval is [lo, hi) with hi exclusive, the loop runs while lo < hi, and the keep-branch is hi = mid (mid stays a candidate), never hi = mid - 1. The test nums[mid] < target sends lo past mid; anything else keeps mid in play. The loop ends with lo == hi pointing exactly at the boundary.

frames.caption: LowerBound: half-open interval lo = 0, hi = 6 (hi is exclusive — the "insert at end" answer).
frames.note: LowerBound finds the FIRST index i with nums[i] >= target — the left edge of the duplicate run. Its half-open interval is [lo, hi) with hi exclusive, the loop runs while lo < hi, and the keep-branch is hi = mid (mid stays a candidate), never hi = mid - 1. The test nums[mid] < target sends lo past mid; anything else keeps mid in play. The loop ends with lo == hi pointing exactly at the boundary.

frames.caption: mid = 0 + (6 - 0)/2 = 3. Probe nums[3] = 8 against 8.
frames.note: Compute mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2, never (lo + hi) / 2 — the naive sum can overflow a 32-bit int for large indices (the bug that hid in Java's library for years). Because mid floors toward lo and the branches are lo = mid + 1 / hi = mid, the interval always shrinks, so each probe discards half the candidates: O(log n).

frames.caption: nums[3] = 8 >= 8: mid is a candidate. Keep it and shrink the right: hi = mid = 3.
frames.note: LowerBound finds the FIRST index i with nums[i] >= target — the left edge of the duplicate run. Its half-open interval is [lo, hi) with hi exclusive, the loop runs while lo < hi, and the keep-branch is hi = mid (mid stays a candidate), never hi = mid - 1. The test nums[mid] < target sends lo past mid; anything else keeps mid in play. The loop ends with lo == hi pointing exactly at the boundary.

frames.caption: mid = 0 + (3 - 0)/2 = 1. Probe nums[1] = 7 against 8.
frames.note: Compute mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2, never (lo + hi) / 2 — the naive sum can overflow a 32-bit int for large indices (the bug that hid in Java's library for years). Because mid floors toward lo and the branches are lo = mid + 1 / hi = mid, the interval always shrinks, so each probe discards half the candidates: O(log n).

frames.caption: nums[1] = 7 < 8: too small. The boundary is to the right — discard mid and left: lo = mid + 1 = 2.
frames.note: LowerBound finds the FIRST index i with nums[i] >= target — the left edge of the duplicate run. Its half-open interval is [lo, hi) with hi exclusive, the loop runs while lo < hi, and the keep-branch is hi = mid (mid stays a candidate), never hi = mid - 1. The test nums[mid] < target sends lo past mid; anything else keeps mid in play. The loop ends with lo == hi pointing exactly at the boundary.

frames.caption: mid = 2 + (3 - 2)/2 = 2. Probe nums[2] = 7 against 8.
frames.note: Compute mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2, never (lo + hi) / 2 — the naive sum can overflow a 32-bit int for large indices (the bug that hid in Java's library for years). Because mid floors toward lo and the branches are lo = mid + 1 / hi = mid, the interval always shrinks, so each probe discards half the candidates: O(log n).

frames.caption: nums[2] = 7 < 8: too small. The boundary is to the right — discard mid and left: lo = mid + 1 = 3.
frames.note: LowerBound finds the FIRST index i with nums[i] >= target — the left edge of the duplicate run. Its half-open interval is [lo, hi) with hi exclusive, the loop runs while lo < hi, and the keep-branch is hi = mid (mid stays a candidate), never hi = mid - 1. The test nums[mid] < target sends lo past mid; anything else keeps mid in play. The loop ends with lo == hi pointing exactly at the boundary.

frames.caption: lo == hi: LowerBound stops. first = 3 — the left edge of the run of 8s.
frames.note: LowerBound finds the FIRST index i with nums[i] >= target — the left edge of the duplicate run. Its half-open interval is [lo, hi) with hi exclusive, the loop runs while lo < hi, and the keep-branch is hi = mid (mid stays a candidate), never hi = mid - 1. The test nums[mid] < target sends lo past mid; anything else keeps mid in play. The loop ends with lo == hi pointing exactly at the boundary.

frames.caption: Guard: first (3) != nums.Length (6) and nums[3] = 8 == 8. Target is present — continue.
frames.note: You MUST check nums[first], not just the index. LowerBound returns an insertion point even when the target is absent (it points at the next-larger value, or n if all are smaller). Skipping this check would report a phantom range for a value that was never there.

frames.caption: Step 2 — UpperBound(8) finds the FIRST index with nums[i] > 8. Reset lo = 0, hi = 6.
frames.note: UpperBound is LowerBound with one character changed: the test is nums[mid] <= target instead of < target. That makes it find the FIRST index strictly GREATER than target — the index just past the run of equal values. Subtract one and you land on the last occurrence. Same half-open [lo, hi), same hi = mid keep-branch.

frames.caption: mid = 0 + (6 - 0)/2 = 3. Probe nums[3] = 8 against 8.
frames.note: Compute mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2, never (lo + hi) / 2 — the naive sum can overflow a 32-bit int for large indices (the bug that hid in Java's library for years). Because mid floors toward lo and the branches are lo = mid + 1 / hi = mid, the interval always shrinks, so each probe discards half the candidates: O(log n).

frames.caption: nums[3] = 8 <= 8: not past the run yet (note <=, the only difference). lo = mid + 1 = 4.
frames.note: UpperBound is LowerBound with one character changed: the test is nums[mid] <= target instead of < target. That makes it find the FIRST index strictly GREATER than target — the index just past the run of equal values. Subtract one and you land on the last occurrence. Same half-open [lo, hi), same hi = mid keep-branch.

frames.caption: mid = 4 + (6 - 4)/2 = 5. Probe nums[5] = 10 against 8.
frames.note: Compute mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2, never (lo + hi) / 2 — the naive sum can overflow a 32-bit int for large indices (the bug that hid in Java's library for years). Because mid floors toward lo and the branches are lo = mid + 1 / hi = mid, the interval always shrinks, so each probe discards half the candidates: O(log n).

frames.caption: nums[5] = 10 > 8: first index past the run is a candidate. hi = mid = 5.
frames.note: UpperBound is LowerBound with one character changed: the test is nums[mid] <= target instead of < target. That makes it find the FIRST index strictly GREATER than target — the index just past the run of equal values. Subtract one and you land on the last occurrence. Same half-open [lo, hi), same hi = mid keep-branch.

frames.caption: mid = 4 + (5 - 4)/2 = 4. Probe nums[4] = 8 against 8.
frames.note: Compute mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2, never (lo + hi) / 2 — the naive sum can overflow a 32-bit int for large indices (the bug that hid in Java's library for years). Because mid floors toward lo and the branches are lo = mid + 1 / hi = mid, the interval always shrinks, so each probe discards half the candidates: O(log n).

frames.caption: nums[4] = 8 <= 8: not past the run yet (note <=, the only difference). lo = mid + 1 = 5.
frames.note: UpperBound is LowerBound with one character changed: the test is nums[mid] <= target instead of < target. That makes it find the FIRST index strictly GREATER than target — the index just past the run of equal values. Subtract one and you land on the last occurrence. Same half-open [lo, hi), same hi = mid keep-branch.

frames.caption: lo == hi: UpperBound stops at 5 (first index > 8). last = 5 - 1 = 4 — the right edge of the run.
frames.note: UpperBound is LowerBound with one character changed: the test is nums[mid] <= target instead of < target. That makes it find the FIRST index strictly GREATER than target — the index just past the run of equal values. Subtract one and you land on the last occurrence. Same half-open [lo, hi), same hi = mid keep-branch.

frames.caption: Return [first, last] = [3, 4]. The run of 8s occupies indices 3 and 4.
frames.note: Two boundary searches bracket the duplicate run: first = LowerBound (left edge), last = UpperBound - 1 (right edge). Each is O(log n), so the whole range query is O(log n) — far better than scanning outward from a single hit, which degrades to O(n) when the array is one giant run of the target.
